subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the height of a given tower using mathematical modelling.
question_html: Find the height of a given tower using mathematical modelling.
solution_text: Step 1 Given physical situation is to find the height of a given tower. Step 2 Let AB be the given tower in the figure given below. Let PQ be an observer measuring the height of the tower with his eye at P. Let PQ = h and let height of tower be H. Let α be the angle of elevation from the eye of the observer to the top of the tower. Let l = PC = QB Now {tex}\tan \alpha=\frac{\mathrm{AC}}{\mathrm{PC}}=\frac{\mathrm{H}-h}{l}{/tex} or H = h + l tan {tex}\alpha{/tex} ... (1) Step 3 Note that the values of the parameters h, l and {tex}\alpha{/tex} (using sextant) are known to the observer and so (1) gives the solution of the problem. Step 4 In case, if the foot of the tower is not accessible, i.e., when l is not known to the observer, let {tex}\beta{/tex} be the angle of depression from P to the foot B of the tower. So from {tex}\triangle{/tex} PQB, we have {tex}\tan \beta=\frac{\mathrm{PQ}}{\mathrm{QB}}=\frac{h}{l}{/tex} or l = h cot {tex}\beta{/tex} Step 5 is not required in this situation as exact values of the parameters h, l, {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are known.
solution_html:
Step 1 Given physical situation is to find the height of a given tower.
Step 2 Let AB be the given tower in the figure given below. Let PQ be an observer measuring the height of the tower with his eye at P. Let PQ = h and let height of tower be H. Let α be the angle of elevation from the eye of the observer to the top of the tower.

Let l = PC = QB
Now {tex}\tan \alpha=\frac{\mathrm{AC}}{\mathrm{PC}}=\frac{\mathrm{H}-h}{l}{/tex}
or H = h + l tan{tex}\alpha{/tex} ... (1)
Step 3 Note that the values of the parameters h, l and {tex}\alpha{/tex}(using sextant) are known to the observer and so (1) gives the solution of the problem.
Step 4 In case, if the foot of the tower is not accessible, i.e., when l is not known to the observer, let {tex}\beta{/tex} be the angle of depression from P to the foot B of the tower. So from {tex}\triangle{/tex}PQB, we have
{tex}\tan \beta=\frac{\mathrm{PQ}}{\mathrm{QB}}=\frac{h}{l}{/tex} or l = h cot{tex}\beta{/tex}
Step 5 is not required in this situation as exact values of the parameters h, l, {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} are known.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Let a business firm produces three types of products P 1 , P 2 and P 3 that uses three types of raw materials R 1 , R 2 and R 3 . Let the firm has purchase orders from two clients F 1 and F 2 . Considering the situation that the firm has a limited quantity of R 1 , R 2 and R 3 , respectively, prepare a model to determine the quantities of the raw material R 1 , R 2 and R 3 required to meet the purchase orders.
question_html: Let a business firm produces three types of products P1, P2 and P3 that uses three types of raw materials R1, R2 and R3. Let the firm has purchase orders from two clients F1 and F2. Considering the situation that the firm has a limited quantity of R1, R2 and R3, respectively, prepare a model to determine the quantities of the raw material R1, R2 and R3 required to meet the purchase orders.
solution_text: Step 1 The physical situation is well identified in the problem. Step 2 Let A be a matrix that represents purchase orders from the two clients F 1 and F 2 . Then, A is of the form Let B be the matrix that represents the amount of raw materials R 1 , R 2 and R 3 , required to manufacture each unit of the products P 1 , P 2 and P 3 . Then, B is of the form Step 3 Note that the product (which in this case is well defined) of matrices A and B is given by the following matrix Which in fact gives the desired quantities of the raw materials R 1 , R 2 and R 3 to fulfil the purchase orders of the two clients F 1 and F 2 .

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Let a business firm produces three types of products P 1 , P 2 and P 3 that uses three types of raw materials R 1 , R 2 and R 3 . Let the firm has purchase orders from two clients F 1 and F 2 . Considering the situation that the firm has a limited quantity of R 1 , R 2 and R 3 , respectively, prepare a model to determine the quantities of the raw material R 1 , R 2 and R 3 required to meet the purchase orders. In case A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 10 & 15 & 6 \\ 10 & 20 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & 4 & 0 \\ 7 & 9 & 3 \\ 5 & 12 & 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and the available raw materials are 330 units of R 1 , 455 units of R 2 and 140 units of R 3 .
question_html:
Let a business firm produces three types of products P1, P2 and P3 that uses three types of raw materials R1, R2 and R3. Let the firm has purchase orders from two clients F1 and F2. Considering the situation that the firm has a limited quantity of R1, R2 and R3, respectively, prepare a model to determine the quantities of the raw material R1, R2 and R3 required to meet the purchase orders. In case
A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 10 & 15 & 6 \\ 10 & 20 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & 4 & 0 \\ 7 & 9 & 3 \\ 5 & 12 & 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
and the available raw materials are 330 units of R1, 455 units of R2 and 140 units of R3.
solution_text: Note that AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10 & 15 & 6 \\ 10 & 20 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & 4 & 0 \\ 7 & 9 & 3 \\ 5 & 12 & 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = This clearly shows that to meet the purchase order of F 1 and F 2 , the raw material required is 335 units of R 1 , 467 units of R 2 and 147 units of R 3 which is much more than the available raw material. Since the amount of raw material required to manufacture each unit of the three products is fixed, we can either ask for an increase in the available raw material or we may ask the clients to reduce their orders.
solution_html:
Note that
AB = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10 & 15 & 6 \\ 10 & 20 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 3 & 4 & 0 \\ 7 & 9 & 3 \\ 5 & 12 & 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
= 
This clearly shows that to meet the purchase order of F1 and F2, the raw material required is 335 units of R1, 467 units of R2 and 147 units of R3 which is much more than the available raw material. Since the amount of raw material required to manufacture each unit of the three products is fixed, we can either ask for an increase in the available raw material or we may ask the clients to reduce their orders.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Suppose P 1 , P 2 , P 3 and R 1 , R 2 , R 3 . Let a business firm produces three types of products P 1 , P 2 and P 3 that uses three types of raw materials R 1 , R 2 and R 3 . Let the firm has purchase orders from two clients F 1 and F 2 . Let the firm has 330 units of R 1 , 455 units of R 2 and 140 units of R 3 available with it and let the number of raw materials R 1 , R 2 and R 3 required to manufacture each unit of the three products is given by B = {tex}\left.\begin{array}{c|ccc} & \mathrm{R}_{1} & \mathrm{R}_{2} & \mathrm{R}_{3} \\ \mathrm{P}_{1} & 3 & 4 & 0 \\ \mathrm{P}_{2} & 7 & 9 & 3 \\ \mathrm{P}_{3} & 5 & 12 & 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} How many units of each product is to be made so as to utilise the full available raw material?
question_html:
Suppose P1, P2, P3 and R1, R2, R3. Let a business firm produces three types of products P1, P2 and P3 that uses three types of raw materials R1, R2 and R3. Let the firm has purchase orders from two clients F1 and F2. Let the firm has 330 units of R1, 455 units of R2 and 140 units of R3 available with it and let the number of raw materials R1, R2 and R3 required to manufacture each unit of the three products is given by
B = {tex}\left.\begin{array}{c|ccc} & \mathrm{R}_{1} & \mathrm{R}_{2} & \mathrm{R}_{3} \\ \mathrm{P}_{1} & 3 & 4 & 0 \\ \mathrm{P}_{2} & 7 & 9 & 3 \\ \mathrm{P}_{3} & 5 & 12 & 7 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
How many units of each product is to be made so as to utilise the full available raw material?
solution_text: Step 1 The situation is easily identifiable. Step 2 Suppose the firm produces x units of P 1 , y units of P 2 and z units of P 3 . Since product P 1 requires 3 units of R 1 , P 2 requires 7 units of R 1 and P 3 requires 5 units of R 1 (observe matrix B) and the total number of units, of R 1 , available is 330, we have 3x + 7y + 5z = 330 (for raw material R 1 ) Similarly, we have 4x + 9y + 12z = 455 (for raw material R 2 ) and 3y + 7z = 140 (for raw material R 3 ) This system of equations can be expressed in matrix form as {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 7 & 5 \\ 4 & 9 & 12 \\ 0 & 3 & 7 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 330 \\ 455 \\ 140 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Step 3 Using elementary row operations, we obtain {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 20 \\ 35 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} This gives x = 20, y = 35 and z = 5. Thus, the firm can produce 20 units of P 1 , 35 units of P 2 and 5 units of P 3 to make full use of its available raw material.
solution_html:
Step 1 The situation is easily identifiable.
Step 2 Suppose the firm produces x units of P1, y units of P2 and z units of P3. Since product P1 requires 3 units of R1, P2 requires 7 units of R1 and P3 requires 5 units of R1 (observe matrix B) and the total number of units, of R1, available is 330, we have
3x + 7y + 5z = 330 (for raw material R1)
Similarly, we have
4x + 9y + 12z = 455 (for raw material R2)
and 3y + 7z = 140 (for raw material R3)
This system of equations can be expressed in matrix form as
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 3 & 7 & 5 \\ 4 & 9 & 12 \\ 0 & 3 & 7 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 330 \\ 455 \\ 140 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
Step 3 Using elementary row operations, we obtain
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} 20 \\ 35 \\ 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
This gives x = 20, y = 35 and z = 5. Thus, the firm can produce 20 units of P1, 35 units of P2 and 5 units of P3 to make full use of its available raw material.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A manufacturer of medicines is preparing a production plan of medicines M 1 and M 2 . There are sufficient raw materials available to make 20000 bottles of M 1 and 40000 bottles of M 2 , but there are only 45000 bottles into which either of the medicines can be put. Further, it takes 3 hours to prepare enough material to fill 1000 bottles of M 1 , it takes 1 hour to prepare enough material to fill 1000 bottles of M 2 and there are 66 hours available for this operation. The profit is ₹8 per bottle for M 1 and ₹7 per bottle for M 2 . How should the manufacturer schedule his/her production in order to maximise profit?
question_html: A manufacturer of medicines is preparing a production plan of medicines M1 and M2. There are sufficient raw materials available to make 20000 bottles of M1 and 40000 bottles of M2, but there are only 45000 bottles into which either of the medicines can be put. Further, it takes 3 hours to prepare enough material to fill 1000 bottles of M1, it takes 1 hour to prepare enough material to fill 1000 bottles of M2 and there are 66 hours available for this operation. The profit is ₹8 per bottle for M1 and ₹7 per bottle for M2. How should the manufacturer schedule his/her production in order to maximise profit?
solution_text: Step 1 To find the number of bottles of M 1 and M 2 in order to maximise the profit under the given hypotheses. Step 2 Let x be the number of bottles of type M1 medicine and y be the number of bottles of type M 2 medicine. Since profit is ₹8 per bottle for M 1 and ₹7 per bottle for M 2 , therefore the objective function (which is to be maximised) is given by Z {tex}\equiv{/tex} Z (x, y) = 8x + 7y The objective function is to be maximised subject to the constraints x {tex}\le{/tex} 20000, y {tex}\le{/tex} 40000, x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 45000, 3x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 66000, x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 ......(i) Step 3 The shaded region OPQRST is the feasible region for the constraints (i) (In a figure). The co-ordinates of vertices O, P, Q, R, S and T are (0, 0), (20000, 0), (20000, 6000), (10500, 34500), (5000, 40000) and (0, 40000), respectively. Note that Z at P (0, 0) = 0 Z at P (20000, 0) = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 20000 = 160000 Z at Q (20000, 6000) = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 20000 + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 6000 = 202000 Z at R (10500, 34500) = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10500 + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 34500 = 325500 Z at S = (5000, 40000) = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 5000 + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 40000 = 320000 Z at T = (0, 40000) = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 40000 = 280000 Now observe that the profit is maximum at x = 10500 and y = 34500 and the maximum profit is ₹325500. Hence, the manufacturer should produce ₹10500 bottles of M 1 medicine and ₹34500 bottles of M 2 medicine in order to get maximum profit of ₹325500.
solution_html:
Step 1 To find the number of bottles of M1 and M2 in order to maximise the profit under the given hypotheses.
Step 2 Let x be the number of bottles of type M1 medicine and y be the number of bottles of type M2 medicine. Since profit is ₹8 per bottle for M1 and ₹7 per bottle for M2, therefore the objective function (which is to be maximised) is given by
Z {tex}\equiv{/tex} Z (x, y) = 8x + 7y
The objective function is to be maximised subject to the constraints
x {tex}\le{/tex} 20000, y {tex}\le{/tex} 40000, x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 45000, 3x + y {tex}\le{/tex} 66000, x {tex}\ge{/tex} 0, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 ......(i)
Step 3 The shaded region OPQRST is the feasible region for the constraints (i) (In a figure). The co-ordinates of vertices O, P, Q, R, S and T are (0, 0), (20000, 0), (20000, 6000), (10500, 34500), (5000, 40000) and (0, 40000), respectively.
 
Note that
Z at P (0, 0) = 0
Z at P (20000, 0) = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 20000 = 160000
Z at Q (20000, 6000) = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 20000 + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 6000 = 202000
Z at R (10500, 34500) = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10500 + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 34500 = 325500
Z at S = (5000, 40000) = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 5000 + 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 40000 = 320000
Z at T = (0, 40000) = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 40000 = 280000
Now observe that the profit is maximum at x = 10500 and y = 34500 and the maximum profit is ₹325500. Hence, the manufacturer should produce ₹10500 bottles of M1 medicine and ₹34500 bottles of M2 medicine in order to get maximum profit of ₹325500.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Suppose a company plans to produce a new product that incur some costs (fixed and variable) and let the company plans to sell the product at a fixed price. Prepare a mathematical model to examine the profitability.
question_html: Suppose a company plans to produce a new product that incur some costs (fixed and variable) and let the company plans to sell the product at a fixed price. Prepare a mathematical model to examine the profitability.
solution_text: Step 1 Situation is clearly identifiable. Step 2 Formulation: We are given that the costs are of two types: fixed and variable. The fixed costs are independent of the number of units produced (e.g., rent and rates), while the variable costs increase with the number of units produced (e.g., material). Initially, we assume that the variable costs are directly proportional to the number of units produced — this should simplify our model. The company earn a certain amount of money by selling its products and wants to ensure that it is maximum. For convenience, we assume that all units produced are sold immediately. Let x = number of units produced and sold C = total cost of production (in rupees) I = income from sales (in rupees) P = profit (in rupees) Our assumptions above state that C consists of two parts: fixed cost = a (in rupees), variable cost = b (rupees/unit produced). Then C = a + bx ....(i) Also, income I depends on selling price s (rupees/unit) Thus I = sx .....(ii) The profit P is then the difference between income and costs. So P = I – C = sx – (a + bx) = (s – b) x – a ......(iii) We now have a mathematical model of the relationships (i) to (iii) between the variables x, C, I, P, a, b, s. These variables may be classified as: independent - x, dependent - C, I, P, parameters - a, b, s The manufacturer, knowing x, a, b, s can determine P. Step 3 From (iii), we can observe that for the break even point (i.e., make neither profit nor loss), he must have P = 0, i.e., x = {tex}\frac{a}{s-b}{/tex} units. Steps 4 and 5 In view of the break even point, one may conclude that if the company produces few units, i.e., less than x = {tex}\frac{a}{s-b}{/tex} units, then the company will suffer loss and if it produces large number of units, i.e., much more than {tex}\frac{a}{s-b}{/tex} units, then it can make huge profit. Further, if the break even point proves to be unrealistic, then another model could be tried or the assumptions regarding cash flow may be modified.
solution_html:
Step 1 Situation is clearly identifiable.
Step 2 Formulation: We are given that the costs are of two types: fixed and variable. The fixed costs are independent of the number of units produced (e.g., rent and rates), while the variable costs increase with the number of units produced (e.g., material). Initially, we assume that the variable costs are directly proportional to the number of units produced — this should simplify our model. The company earn a certain amount of money by selling its products and wants to ensure that it is maximum. For convenience, we assume that all units produced are sold immediately.
Let x = number of units produced and sold C = total cost of production (in rupees) I = income from sales (in rupees) P = profit (in rupees)
Our assumptions above state that C consists of two parts: 
fixed cost = a (in rupees), variable cost = b (rupees/unit produced).
Then C = a + bx ....(i)
Also, income I depends on selling price s (rupees/unit)
Thus I = sx .....(ii)
The profit P is then the difference between income and costs. So
P = I – C = sx – (a + bx) = (s – b) x – a ......(iii)
We now have a mathematical model of the relationships (i) to (iii) between the variables x, C, I, P, a, b, s. These variables may be classified as:
independent - x, dependent - C, I, P, parameters - a, b, s
The manufacturer, knowing x, a, b, s can determine P.
Step 3 From (iii), we can observe that for the break even point (i.e., make neither profit nor loss), he must have P = 0, i.e., x = {tex}\frac{a}{s-b}{/tex} units.
Steps 4 and 5 In view of the break even point, one may conclude that if the company produces few units, i.e., less than x = {tex}\frac{a}{s-b}{/tex} units, then the company will suffer loss and if it produces large number of units, i.e., much more than {tex}\frac{a}{s-b}{/tex} units, then it can make huge profit. Further, if the break even point proves to be unrealistic, then another model could be tried or the assumptions regarding cash flow may be modified.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Let a tank contains 1000 litres of brine which contains 250 g of salt per litre. Brine containing 200 g of salt per litre flows into the tank at the rate of 25 litres per minute and the mixture flows out at the same rate. Assume that the mixture is kept uniform all the time by stirring. What would be the amount of salt in the tank at any time t?
question_html: Let a tank contains 1000 litres of brine which contains 250 g of salt per litre. Brine containing 200 g of salt per litre flows into the tank at the rate of 25 litres per minute and the mixture flows out at the same rate. Assume that the mixture is kept uniform all the time by stirring. What would be the amount of salt in the tank at any time t?
solution_text: Step 1 The situation is easily identifiable. Step 2 Let y = y (t) denote the amount of salt (in kg) in the tank at time t (in minutes) after the inflow, outflow starts. Further, assume that y is a differentiable function. When t = 0, i.e., before the inflow–outflow of the brine starts, y = 250 g {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 = 250 kg Note that the change in y occurs due to the inflow, outflow of the mixture. Now the inflow of brine brings salt into the tank at the rate of 5 kg per minute (as 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 g = 5 kg) and the outflow of brine takes the salt out of the tank at the rate of {tex}25\left(\frac{y}{1000}\right)=\frac{y}{40}{/tex} kg per minute (as at time t, the salt in the tank is {tex}\frac{y}{1000}{/tex} kg) Thus, the rate of change of salt with respect to t is given by {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=5-\frac{y}{40}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}+\frac{1}{40} y=5{/tex} ....(i) This gives a mathematical model for the given problem. Step 3 Equation (i) is a linear equation and can be easily solved. The solution of (i) is given by {tex}y e^{\frac{t}{40}}=200 e^{\frac{t}{40}}+C \text { or } y(t)=200+\mathrm{C} e^{-\frac{t}{40}}{/tex} .....(ii) where, c is the constant of integration. Note that when t = 0, y = 250. Therefore, 250 = 200 + C or C = 50 Then (ii) reduces to {tex}y=200+50 e^{-\frac{t}{40}}{/tex} ....(iii) or {tex}\frac{y-200}{50}=e^{-\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} or {tex}e^{\frac{i}{40}}=\frac{50}{y-200}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}t=40 \log _{e}\left(\frac{50}{y-200}\right){/tex} ....(iv) Here, the equation (4) gives the time t at which the salt in tank is y kg. Step 4 Since {tex}e^{-\frac{t}{40}}{/tex} is always positive, from (iii), we conclude that y > 200 at all times. Thus, the minimum amount of salt content in the tank is 200 kg. Also, from (iv), we conclude that t > 0 if and only if 0 < y – 200 < 50 i.e., if and only if 200 < y < 250 i.e., the amount of salt content in the tank after the start of inflow and outflow of the brine is between 200 kg and 250 kg.
solution_html:
Step 1 The situation is easily identifiable.
Step 2 Let y = y (t) denote the amount of salt (in kg) in the tank at time t (in minutes) after the inflow, outflow starts. Further, assume that y is a differentiable function.
When t = 0, i.e., before the inflow–outflow of the brine starts,
y = 250 g {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 = 250 kg
Note that the change in y occurs due to the inflow, outflow of the mixture.
Now the inflow of brine brings salt into the tank at the rate of 5 kg per minute (as 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 g = 5 kg) and the outflow of brine takes the salt out of the tank at the rate of {tex}25\left(\frac{y}{1000}\right)=\frac{y}{40}{/tex} kg per minute (as at time t, the salt in the tank is {tex}\frac{y}{1000}{/tex} kg)
Thus, the rate of change of salt with respect to t is given by
{tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=5-\frac{y}{40}{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}+\frac{1}{40} y=5{/tex} ....(i)
This gives a mathematical model for the given problem.
Step 3 Equation (i) is a linear equation and can be easily solved. The solution of (i) is given by
{tex}y e^{\frac{t}{40}}=200 e^{\frac{t}{40}}+C \text { or } y(t)=200+\mathrm{C} e^{-\frac{t}{40}}{/tex} .....(ii)
where, c is the constant of integration.
Note that when t = 0, y = 250. Therefore, 250 = 200 + C
or C = 50
Then (ii) reduces to
{tex}y=200+50 e^{-\frac{t}{40}}{/tex} ....(iii)
or {tex}\frac{y-200}{50}=e^{-\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} 
or {tex}e^{\frac{i}{40}}=\frac{50}{y-200}{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}t=40 \log _{e}\left(\frac{50}{y-200}\right){/tex} ....(iv)
Here, the equation (4) gives the time t at which the salt in tank is y kg.
Step 4 Since {tex}e^{-\frac{t}{40}}{/tex} is always positive, from (iii), we conclude that y > 200 at all times. Thus, the minimum amount of salt content in the tank is 200 kg.
Also, from (iv), we conclude that t > 0 if and only if 0 < y – 200 < 50 i.e., if and only if 200 < y < 250 i.e., the amount of salt content in the tank after the start of inflow and outflow of the brine is between 200 kg and 250 kg.
